input.prompt:
I have a .NET MAUI app with a ContentPage containing labels, buttons, and
a background color. Everything is hardcoded to light colors right now.
I want the app to automatically switch between light and dark mode based
on the device's system theme. Show me how to use AppThemeBinding to make
this page theme-aware in XAML and explain the C# alternative.

I have a .NET MAUI app with a ContentPage containing labels, buttons, and
a background color. Everything is hardcoded to light colors right now.
I want the app to automatically switch between light and dark mode based
on the device's system theme. Show me how to use AppThemeBinding to make

input.initialPrompt:
I have a .NET MAUI app with a ContentPage containing labels, buttons, and
a background color. Everything is hardcoded to light colors right now.
I want the app to automatically switch between light and dark mode based
on the device's system theme. Show me how to use AppThemeBinding to make
this page theme-aware in XAML and explain the C# alternative.

data.content:
Here's how to make your MAUI page theme-aware:

## XAML ŌĆö `AppThemeBinding`

```xml
<ContentPage xmlns="http://schemas.microsoft.com/dotnet/2021/maui"
             xmlns:x="http://schemas.microsoft.com/winfx/2009/xaml"
             x:Class="MyApp.MainPage"
             BackgroundColor="{AppThemeBinding Light=#FFFFFF, Dark=#1E1E1E}">

    <VerticalStackLayout Padding="20" Spacing="16">

        <Label Text="Welcome"
               FontSize="28"
               TextColor="{AppThemeBinding Light=#000000, Dark=#E0E0E0}" />

        <Label Text="This page adapts to your system theme."
               FontSize="16"
               TextColor="{AppThemeBinding Light=#444444, Dark=#BBBBBB}" />

        <Button Text="Get Started"
                BackgroundColor="{AppThemeBinding Light=#6200EE, Dark=#BB86FC}"
                TextColor="{AppThemeBinding Light=#FFFFFF, Dark=#000000}" />

    </VerticalStackLayout>
</ContentPage>
```

`AppThemeBinding` reacts to the OS theme automatically ŌĆö no event wiring needed.

---

## C# alternative ŌĆö `SetAppTheme<T>()`

The equivalent in code-behind or C# markup:

```csharp
// Extension method on any BindableObject
var page = new ContentPage();
page.SetAppTheme<Color>(
    ContentPage.BackgroundColorProperty,
    Colors.White,   // Light
    Color.FromArgb("#1E1E1E")); // Dark

var label = new Label { Text = "Welcome", FontSize = 28 };
label.SetAppTheme<Color>(
    Label.TextColorProperty,
    Colors.Black,
    Color.FromArgb("#E0E0E0"));

var button = new Button { Text = "Get Started" };
button.SetAppTheme<Color>(
    Button.BackgroundColorProperty,
    Color.FromArgb("#6200EE"),
    Color.FromArgb("#BB86FC"));
```

---

## Key points

| Topic | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Trigger** | Tracks `Application.RequestedTheme` (`Light` / `Dark` / `Unspecified`) |
| **Live updates** | Values swap instantly when the user toggles the OS theme |
| **Resources alternative** | You can also define `AppThemeBinding` inside a `ResourceDictionary` style so every `Label`/`Button` picks it up globally |
| **Override** | Set `Application.Current.UserAppTheme` to force a specific theme, ignoring the system setting |

Both approaches are functionally identical ŌĆö pick XAML for declarative UI, C# for programmatic/dynamic scenarios.
